source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 7.1 General (pp. 98-99)

questions.q: What is the difference between a user's primary group and their supplementary groups?
options: Every user has exactly one primary group; supplementary groups are additional groups they also belong to
The primary group is optional; supplementary groups are mandatory
A user can have several primary groups but only one supplementary group
There is no difference
questions.why: Each user has one primary group and may additionally be a member of one or more supplementary groups.

questions.q: What do UID and GID stand for?
options: UID = User Identifier (unique user ID); GID = Group Identifier (unique group ID)
UID = Unix ID; GID = Global ID
UID = Unique Item Descriptor; GID = Group Item Descriptor
UID = User Index; GID = Generic ID
questions.why: The kernel manages users and groups by their unique numeric UID and GID.

questions.q: Which UID makes an account the Super Admin?
options: uid=0 (the root user is the usual holder, but any uid=0 account is Super Admin)
uid=999
Any uid below 1000
questions.why: The kernel treats uid=0 as the Super Admin - it need not literally be named root, as long as its UID is 0.

questions.q: Which UID range identifies a normal (regular) user account?
options: UID greater than or equal to 1000
UID between 201 and 999
UID equal to 0
UID between 1 and 200
questions.why: root is uid=0, system/service accounts use 201-999, and regular login users are uid >= 1000.

questions.q: Which of these is the recommended way to change user and group information?
options: Use the administration commands (useradd, usermod, groupadd, ...) rather than editing the files by hand
Edit /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow directly with a text editor
Only ever edit /etc/group
Reboot into single-user mode to make any change
questions.why: The guide warns you to always use the admin commands instead of manually editing files like /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group and /etc/gshadow.

questions.q: Which of these files is NOT one of the standard user/group management files listed?
options: /etc/hostname
/etc/passwd
/etc/shadow
/etc/login.defs
questions.why: The user/group files are /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group, /etc/gshadow, /etc/skel/, /etc/default/useradd and /etc/login.defs; /etc/hostname is unrelated.
